q: Using Naegele's rule, if the last menstrual period was June 10, the estimated date of delivery is:
rationale: Naegele's rule: take first day of LMP, add 7 days, subtract 3 months. June 10 + 7 days = June 17. June - 3 months = March 17.

q: Ideal weight gain during pregnancy is:
rationale: Ideal weight gain: 28 lbs ± 3 = 25-31 lbs. 1st trimester: 1 lb/month (3 lbs total). 2nd and 3rd trimesters: 1 lb/week.

q: The fundus can be palpated at the umbilicus between weeks:
rationale: Fundal height cannot be palpated until week 12 (midway between umbilicus and pubic symphysis). The fundus is at the umbilicus between 20-22 weeks.

q: The truest, most valid sign that a woman is in labor is:
rationale: The truest, most valid sign of labor is the onset of regular/progressive contractions. This is more reliable than other signs.

q: For a prolapsed umbilical cord, the nurse should:
options: Push the fetal head off the cord and position mother in knee-chest or Trendelenburg
rationale: Prolapsed cord: PUSH and POSITION. Push the fetal head off the cord and position mother in knee-chest or Trendelenburg. Prepare for C-section.

q: The LION protocol for OB complications stands for:
options: Left side, IV, Oxygen, Notify HCP
rationale: LION: Left side (place mother on left side), IV, Oxygen, Notify HCP. Used for tetany, maternal hypertension, vena cava syndrome, toxemia, uterine rupture. Stop Pitocin FIRST if running.

q: Variable decelerations on fetal monitoring indicate:
rationale: Variable (VERY) decelerations = VERY BAD = prolapsed cord. Nursing intervention: PUSH and POSITION. Fetal tracings starting with 'L' are bad (do LION). Variable = very bad = push/position.

q: Late decelerations on fetal monitoring require which intervention?
rationale: Late decelerations are BAD (starts with 'L'). Do LION: Left side, IV, Oxygen, Notify HCP. Stop Pitocin if running. Late decelerations indicate uteroplacental insufficiency.

q: Early decelerations on fetal monitoring indicate:
rationale: Early decelerations are NORMAL - no big deal. Just document the finding. They result from head compression during contractions.

q: The nurse should stop Pitocin if contractions are longer than _____ seconds or closer than _____ minutes:
rationale: Stop Pitocin if contractions are longer than 90 seconds or closer than 2 minutes. This indicates uterine tetany/hyperstimulation.

q: In the 4th stage of labor, the nurse assesses every 15 minutes for:
options: V/S, fundus, perineal pads, roll patient over
rationale: 4th stage (Recovery, 2 hours until bleeding stops): Assess every 15 minutes: V/S (shock assessment), Fundus (if boggy, massage; if displaced, catheterize), Perineal pads, Roll patient over to check for bleeding.

q: Postpartum assessment uses the mnemonic 'BUBBLE HEAD.' The three BIG things on the exam are:
rationale: The 3 big postpartum exam topics are: Fundus, Lochia, and Thrombophlebitis. Best way to check for thrombophlebitis: measure bilateral calf circumference (not Homan's sign).

q: Caput succedaneum is characterized by edema that:
options: Crosses suture lines and is symmetrical
Only occurs on one side of the head
rationale: Caput Succedaneum (CS) = Crosses Suture lines and is Caput Symmetrical. It is a normal finding from mechanical trauma during birth and resolves spontaneously.

q: Physiologic jaundice in a newborn appears:
rationale: Physiologic jaundice is NORMAL and appears AFTER 24 hours. Pathologic jaundice appears BEFORE 24 hours and is abnormal. This is a key distinction on the NCLEX.

q: Terbutaline (Brethine) is a tocolytic used to:
rationale: Tocolytics STOP contractions/labor. Terbutaline (Brethine) is given to women in premature labor that must be stopped. Side effect: maternal tachycardia.

q: Betamethasone is given during premature labor to:
rationale: Betamethasone (a steroid) is given to mature fetal lungs. Can be repeated as long as baby is in utero. Side effect: increased glucose (steroid effect). Surfactant (Survanta) is given to baby AFTER birth.

q: The most common and favorable fetal presentation is:
options: ROA (right occiput anterior) or LOA (left occiput anterior)
rationale: Most common presentation is ROA or LOA. Pick ROA before LOA. A vertical lie is compatible with vaginal birth. A transverse (perpendicular) lie = Trouble = C-section.

q: Prenatal visits are scheduled:
options: Monthly until 28 weeks, biweekly 28-36, weekly after 36
rationale: Prenatal visits: once monthly until week 28, every other week 28-36, once weekly after week 36 until delivery or week 42 (whichever comes first).

q: Heparin anticoagulant therapy is monitored by which lab test?
rationale: Heparin is monitored by PTT. It can be given IV or SQ (except Lovenox). Antidote: Protamine sulfate. Heparin CAN be used during pregnancy (Class C). Coumadin uses PT/INR.

q: Coumadin (warfarin) is classified as which pregnancy category?
rationale: Coumadin is Category X - CANNOT be used during pregnancy. It is teratogenic. Antidote: Vitamin K. Monitored by PT/INR. Only given PO. Takes 4-5 days to work.
